mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in the Azure cloud. It is ideal for users looking to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Azure's ecosystem and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install Azure CLI
steps.description:
The Azure CLI is required to interact with Azure resources and manage your AKS cluster.

```bash

curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

```

> This command installs the Azure CLI on Debian-based systems.

steps.title: Create an AKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new AKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

```

> Replace 'myResourceGroup' and 'myAKSCluster' with your desired names.
commands: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the new AKS cluster by updating the kubeconfig file.

```bash

az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster

```

> This command merges the AKS cluster credentials into your kubeconfig.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your AKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring and autoscaling for your AKS cluster to ensure optimal performance.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

az aks update --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

```

> This command enables monitoring for your AKS cluster.
> This command enables cluster autoscaling.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

uninstall.title: Delete AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the AKS cluster and all associated resources, which may result in data loss.

```bash

az aks delete --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --yes --no-wait

```

> This command will delete the cluster and all associated resources.

uninstall.title: Remove Resource Group
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the resource group, which will remove all resources within it.

```bash

az group delete --name myResourceGroup --yes --no-wait

```

> Ensure you have backed up any necessary data before running this.

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Backup your current configuration, upgrade the AKS cluster, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get nodes -o wide > backup-nodes.txt

az aks upgrade --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Backup current node configuration.
> This command upgrades the AKS cluster to the specified version.
> Verify that all nodes are in 'Ready' state after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to get credentials for cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the AKS cluster is not created or the resource group is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the resource group and cluster name, then recreate the credentials using 'az aks get-credentials'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the requested node count exceeds the available resources in the specified region.

**Fix:**
Reduce the node count or choose a different region with more available resources.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl get nodes returns 'NotReady'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the nodes are still initializing or if there are issues with the underlying infrastructure.

**Fix:**
Check the AKS cluster status with 'az aks show --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster' and troubleshoot accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'The operation could not be completed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur due to network issues or misconfigured permissions.

**Fix:**
Ensure that your Azure CLI is authenticated and has the necessary permissions to manage the AKS resources.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.31, improved integration with Azure Monitor.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.28.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure that all workloads are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see output from 'kubectl get nodes' showing all nodes in 'Ready' state, confirming that your AKS cluster is operational.
codeSnippets: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus
az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
